findings.description: The `index` dict built from the header is never used; field extraction uses hardcoded positional indices (row[0]..row[3]), so any CSV whose columns are not in the exact order id/name/amount/email will silently assign wrong values to fields — dead validation code that gives a false sense of column-order independence.

Positional indexing row[0]..row[3] ignores the header-derived `index` map; a CSV with columns reordered (e.g. email first) will misassign data silently or raise an unguarded ValueError/IndexError with no row-number context.

count_rows counts raw newlines, not CSV records; any CSV with quoted multi-line field values will overcount rows, returning a number larger than the actual record count, which is a silent wrong answer for any caller relying on this value.
count_rows returns -1 when the file is empty (0 lines minus 1), which is a nonsensical negative count; callers expecting a non-negative row count will receive an invalid value with no error raised.
count_rows opens the file without a `with` statement, leaking the file descriptor if any exception is raised before readlines() returns.

If `filename` is an empty string, os.path.basename('') returns '' and os.path.join(export_dir, '') evaluates to the directory path; opening a directory for writing raises IsADirectoryError with no guard or meaningful error message.

The email validity check `'@' in record.email` accepts trivially invalid values such as '@', '@@', or 'a@' as valid emails; if downstream consumers treat stream_valid output as having been validated, they will process structurally invalid emails.

Rows with fewer than 4 columns raise an unguarded IndexError from positional access row[0]..row[3]; the exception carries no row number or offending content, making debugging in production silent and opaque.
